 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.1","title":"Onboarding: Interactive rch setup Wizard","description":"## Problem
First-time setup requires users to:
1. Know to create config directory
2. Manually edit workers.toml
3. Understand SSH key paths
4. Choose daemon start method
5. Install hook manually

This takes 30+ minutes and assumes Unix expertise.

## Solution
Create interactive wizard: `rch setup`
```
$ rch setup

Welcome to RCH Setup!

This wizard will help you configure remote compilation.

Step 1/4: Worker Configuration
Where is your build server?
  Hostname or IP: ▌

What username for SSH?
  Username [ubuntu]: ▌

How many CPU cores does it have?
  Cores [8]: 16

Testing SSH connection...
  ✓ Connected to ubuntu@192.168.1.100

Step 2/4: Daemon Configuration
  ✓ Created ~/.config/rch/config.toml
  ✓ Created ~/.config/rch/workers.toml

Step 3/4: Starting Daemon
  ✓ Daemon started (PID 12345)

Step 4/4: Installing Hook
  ✓ Claude Code hook installed

Setup complete! Try it out:
  $ cargo build

Run 'rch doctor' to verify everything is working.
```

## Implementation Details
- Use dialoguer crate for interactive prompts
- Validate SSH connection before saving config
- Create config files atomically
- Start daemon in background
- Run hook install
- Run abbreviated doctor at end

## Error Recovery
- If SSH fails: offer to retry or skip
- If daemon fails: show logs, offer manual mode
- If hook fails: show manual instructions
- Allow resuming partial setup

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/commands.rs - add setup subcommand
- rch/src/main.rs - wire up setup command
- Create rch/src/setup.rs - wizard logic

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Single command gets user to working state
- [ ] SSH tested before config saved
- [ ] Clear progress indicators
- [ ] Errors don't abort - offer recovery
- [ ] Takes <5 minutes for happy path

Added alias and 2 tests.  {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.2","title":"Onboarding: rch workers init for Scaffolded Configuration","description":"## Problem
Worker configuration requires:
1. Creating workers.toml manually
2. Knowing correct TOML syntax
3. Knowing what fields are required vs optional
4. Guessing appropriate values for slots, priority

Users often get syntax wrong or use bad defaults.

## Solution
Add `rch workers init` for guided worker addition:
```
$ rch workers init
Adding a new worker...

Hostname or IP: 192.168.1.100
SSH Username [ubuntu]: devops
SSH Key Path [~/.ssh/id_rsa]: ~/.ssh/worker_key
Worker ID (short name) [worker-1]: gpu-server

Testing connection...
  ✓ SSH connection successful
  ✓ Detected 16 CPU cores
  ✓ Detected Rust 1.84.0-nightly

Recommended slots: 16 (based on CPU cores)
Use recommended? [Y/n]: y

Priority (100 = normal, higher = preferred): 100

✓ Added worker 'gpu-server' to ~/.config/rch/workers.toml
✓ Worker is healthy

Run 'rch workers list' to see all workers.
```

## Features
- Auto-detect CPU cores via SSH
- Auto-detect Rust toolchain
- Validate SSH key path exists
- Test connection before saving
- Offer recommended values
- Append to existing config (don't overwrite)

## Implementation
- SSH to worker to get core count
- Parse /proc/cpuinfo or use nproc
- Check rustc --version
- Append [[workers]] section to TOML

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/commands.rs - add workers init subcommand
- rch/src/main.rs - wire up command

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Prompts for all required fields
- [ ] SSH connection tested before save
- [ ] CPU cores auto-detected
- [ ] Rust toolchain auto-detected
- [ ] Appends to existing config
- [ ] Worker immediately usable after init

```rust
    // Existing workers preserved
}

#[test]

    // Mock SSH response for nproc
}
```

### E2E Tests
```bash

rch workers init
# Answer prompts
# Verify workers.toml created
# Verify worker probed successfully
```

### Logging
- Log each prompt and response
- Log SSH commands executed
- Log config file changes","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:16:16.996643032-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T10:30:28.932407115-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T10:26:51.686171357-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented rch workers init command: 5-step wizard guides users through adding workers with SSH connection testing, auto-detection of CPU cores and Rust version, recommended slot configuration, and appending to workers.toml. Added WorkersAction::Init variant, handler, workers_init function (~250 lines in commands.rs), and 3 CLI tests. All 506 tests pass.","labels":["cli","onboarding","workers"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.2","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:16:17.010516321-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.3","title":"Onboarding: Auto-run Doctor After Hook Installation","description":"## Problem
After installing the hook, users don't know if everything is working. They discover issues only when builds are slow (fell back to local). The first-run experience should validate the entire setup.

## Solution
Auto-run abbreviated doctor after `rch hook install`:
```
$ rch hook install

✓ Hook installed to Claude Code

Running quick health check...
  ✓ Daemon running (PID 12345)
  ✓ 2 workers healthy
  ✓ SSH connectivity verified
  ⚠ Worker 'gpu-1' has circuit breaker open

Setup complete! Your next cargo build will compile remotely.
Issues found: 1 warning (run 'rch doctor' for details)
```

## Implementation Details
- After successful hook install, run doctor checks
- Use abbreviated check set (skip slow network tests)
- Summarize results: passed, warnings, failures
- If failures: suggest `rch doctor` for full report
- If warnings: show inline summary

## Abbreviated Checks
1. Daemon running
2. At least one healthy worker
3. SSH keys accessible
4. Hook status verified

## Full Doctor Checks (for reference)
- Prerequisites (rsync, zstd, ssh)
- Config file validity
- SSH key permissions
- All worker connectivity
- Toolchain alignment

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/commands.rs - modify hook install to call doctor
- rch/src/doctor.rs - add quick_check mode

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Doctor runs automatically after hook install
- [ ] Quick checks complete in <5 seconds
- [ ] Clear summary of setup health
- [ ] Warnings shown inline
- [ ] Failures prompt full doctor run

-{"id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.4","title":"Onboarding: Show Success Message on First Remote Build","description":"## Problem
The hook is intentionally silent. But for first-time users, this creates uncertainty. "Did it actually work? Was that build remote or local?" The first successful remote build should provide confirmation.

## Solution
Track first successful build and show one-time message:
```
$ cargo build
   Compiling myproject v0.1.0
    Finished release [optimized] target(s) in 2.3s

──────────────────────────────────────────────────
🎉 First remote build complete!

Your build ran on 'css' in 2.3s (local would be ~7.5s)
RCH is now working silently in the background.

To see build activity: rch status --jobs
To disable this message: rch config set first_run_complete true
──────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

## Implementation Details
- Store `first_run_complete: false` in config
- After first successful remote build, show message
- Set `first_run_complete: true` to suppress future messages
- Estimate local time from historical data or benchmarks

## Message Content
- Confirm build ran remotely
- Show actual vs estimated local time
- Explain silent operation going forward
- Suggest status command for future visibility

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/config.rs - add first_run_complete field
- rch/src/hook.rs - check flag, show message, set flag

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Message shows only once per installation
- [ ] Shows worker name and timing
- [ ] Can be disabled via config
- [ ] Message goes to stderr
- [ ] Doesn't break output parsing

 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.5","title":"Onboarding: SSH Setup Guidance in Error Messages","description":"## Problem
SSH errors are the #1 blocker for new users. Error messages say "Permission denied (publickey)" but don't explain:
- How to generate SSH keys
- How to copy keys to workers
- How to use SSH agent
- How to debug SSH issues

## Solution
Enhance SSH-related errors with contextual guidance:
```
$ rch workers probe --all

Worker: gpu-1
  Error: SSH connection failed - Permission denied (publickey)

SSH Troubleshooting:
  1. Verify key exists:
     ls -la ~/.ssh/id_rsa

  2. Copy key to worker:
     ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa ubuntu@gpu-1

  3. Test connection manually:
     ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa ubuntu@gpu-1 echo "success"

  4. If using SSH agent:
     eval $(ssh-agent) && ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_rsa

Run 'rch doctor' for comprehensive SSH diagnostics.
```

## Implementation Details
- Detect SSH error type (permission, timeout, host key)
- Provide specific guidance for each error type
- Include actual paths from config
- Suggest debug command with -v flag

## Error Types and Guidance
- Permission denied: key copy instructions
- Connection refused: check host/port
- Connection timeout: check network/firewall
- Host key verification: known_hosts instructions
- Agent forwarding: eval ssh-agent instructions

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/error.rs - add SSH error variants with help
- rch/src/commands.rs - use typed errors in probe
- rch/src/doctor.rs - expand SSH checks with guidance

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SSH errors include troubleshooting steps
- [ ] Steps use actual config values (paths, hosts)
- [ ] Different guidance for different error types
- [ ] Debug command provided
- [ ] Doctor reference for more help
","notes":"## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_ssh_permission_denied_includes_guidance() {
    info\!("TEST START: test_ssh_permission_denied_includes_guidance");
    let err = SshError::PermissionDenied { 
        host: "gpu-1".into(),
        user: "ubuntu".into(),
        key_path: PathBuf::from("/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa"),
    };
    info\!("INPUT: SshError::PermissionDenied for ubuntu@gpu-1");
    let msg = format\!("{}", err);
    info\!("RESULT: Error message:\n{}", msg);
    assert\!(msg.contains("ssh-copy-id"));
    assert\!(msg.contains("ubuntu@gpu-1"));
    assert\!(msg.contains("/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa"));
    info\!("VERIFY: Message includes ssh-copy-id command with actual host/path");
    info\!("TEST PASS: test_ssh_permission_denied_includes_guidance");
}

#[test]
fn test_ssh_timeout_includes_guidance() {
    info\!("TEST START: test_ssh_timeout_includes_guidance");
    let err = SshError::ConnectionTimeout {
        host: "10.0.0.5".into(),
        timeout_secs: 30,
    };
    info\!("INPUT: SshError::ConnectionTimeout for 10.0.0.5");
    let msg = format\!("{}", err);
    info\!("RESULT: Error message:\n{}", msg);
    assert\!(msg.contains("firewall") || msg.contains("network"));
    assert\!(msg.contains("10.0.0.5"));
    info\!("VERIFY: Message includes network troubleshooting guidance");
    info\!("TEST PASS: test_ssh_timeout_includes_guidance");
}

#[test]
fn test_ssh_host_key_includes_guidance() {
    info\!("TEST START: test_ssh_host_key_includes_guidance");
    let err = SshError::HostKeyVerificationFailed { host: "gpu-1".into() };
    info\!("INPUT: SshError::HostKeyVerificationFailed");
    let msg = format\!("{}", err);
    info\!("RESULT: Error message:\n{}", msg);
    assert\!(msg.contains("known_hosts") || msg.contains("ssh-keyscan"));
    info\!("VERIFY: Message includes known_hosts troubleshooting");
    info\!("TEST PASS: test_ssh_host_key_includes_guidance");
}
```

### Integration Tests
```bash
# Test with unreachable host
$ rch workers probe worker-that-does-not-exist 2>&1 | grep -q "Troubleshooting"

# Verify doctor reference included
$ rch workers probe bad-worker 2>&1 | grep -q "rch doctor"
```

All tests must log:
- The specific SSH error type
- The generated error message
- Which troubleshooting guidance is included","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:17:00.544597898-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T18:00:58.010327746-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T18:00:58.010327746-05:00","close_reason":"Added SSH error guidance usage in benchmarks/discovery + improved doctor/key path display","labels":["errors","onboarding","ssh"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.5","depends_on_id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-17T10:17:00.581148126-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-pm5.6","title":"Onboarding: Clear Daemon Lifecycle Documentation and Guidance","description":"## Problem
There are 4 ways to start the daemon:
1. `rchd` (foreground)
2. `rch daemon start` (background)
3. systemd service
4. launchd plist

Users don't know which to use or how they differ. The current documentation is scattered and incomplete.

## Solution
Add clear guidance for daemon lifecycle:
```
$ rch daemon --help

The RCH daemon must be running for remote compilation to work.

Quick Start:
  rch daemon start      Start in background (recommended for daily use)
  rch daemon stop       Stop background daemon
  rch daemon status     Check if running

For Development:
  rchd                  Run in foreground (logs to terminal)
  rchd --verbose        Run with debug logging

For Production:
  sudo systemctl enable rch    Linux: auto-start on boot
  brew services start rch      macOS: auto-start on login

Current Status:
  Daemon: Running (PID 12345)
  Uptime: 2h 15m
  Socket: /tmp/rch.sock
```

## Implementation Details
- Enhance `rch daemon --help` with guidance
- Add `rch daemon status` that shows current state
- Detect and suggest appropriate method for platform
- Warn if daemon not running in relevant commands

## Platform-Specific Guidance
- Linux: systemctl commands
- macOS: launchctl or brew services
- WSL: Background process or Windows service

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/main.rs - enhance daemon subcommand help
- rch/src/commands.rs - add daemon status improvements
- docs/guides/daemon.md - comprehensive documentation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] --help explains all options clearly
- [ ] status shows complete daemon state
- [ ] Platform-appropriate suggestions
- [ ] Warns when daemon not running
- [ ] Auto-start instructions documented

 {"id":"remote_compilation_helper-q3u","title":"Task: Unit Tests for Telemetry Collection","description":"## Overview
Implement unit tests for telemetry collection functions with comprehensive structured logging, specifically the /proc filesystem parsing and metric calculation logic.

## Background and Justification
Telemetry collection reads from /proc (Linux) and calculates derived metrics (CPU %, memory pressure, I/O rates). These calculations must be correct, or worker load information will be inaccurate.

## Logging Infrastructure

### Test Logging Setup
```rust
// rch-telemetry/tests/common/mod.rs
use tracing::{info, debug, warn, error, instrument, span, Level};
use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, EnvFilter, layer::SubscriberExt, util::SubscriberInitExt};
use std::sync::Once;

static INIT: Once = Once::new();

pub fn init_test_logging() {
    INIT.call_once(|| {
        let filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
            .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new("debug"));
        
        tracing_subscriber::registry()
            .with(fmt::layer()
                .with_test_writer()
                .with_target(true)
                .with_file(true)
                .with_line_number(true)
                .with_thread_ids(true)
                .json())
            .with(filter)
            .init();
    });
}

/// Macro for consistent test logging
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! test_log {
    ($phase:expr, $($field:tt)*) => {
        info!(
            test_module = module_path!(),
            test_phase = $phase,
            $($field)*
        )
    };
}
```

## Test Categories with Detailed Logging

### 1. /proc Parsing Tests
```rust
// rch-telemetry/src/collect/cpu_tests.rs

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use crate::tests::common::init_test_logging;
    use tracing::{info, debug, instrument};
    use tracing_test::traced_test;
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_parse_proc_stat() {
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing /proc/stat CPU line parsing"
        );
        
        let sample = r#"cpu  10132153 290696 3084719 46828483 16683 0 25195 0 0 0
cpu0 1393280 32966 572056 13343292 6130 0 17875 0 0 0"#;
        
        debug!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat",
            phase = "input",
            sample_lines = 2,
            sample_preview = &sample[..50]
        );
        
        info!(test = "test_parse_proc_stat", phase = "execute");
        let result = parse_proc_stat(sample);
        
        debug!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat",
            phase = "result",
            is_ok = result.is_ok()
        );
        
        let stats = result.expect("parsing should succeed");
        
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat",
            phase = "assert",
            expected_user = 10132153,
            actual_user = stats.total_user,
            expected_system = 3084719,
            actual_system = stats.total_system,
            expected_idle = 46828483,
            actual_idle = stats.total_idle
        );
        
        assert_eq!(stats.total_user, 10132153, "user time mismatch");
        assert_eq!(stats.total_system, 3084719, "system time mismatch");
        assert_eq!(stats.total_idle, 46828483, "idle time mismatch");
        
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat",
            phase = "complete",
            status = "PASSED"
        );
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_parse_proc_stat_malformed_input() {
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat_malformed_input",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing graceful handling of malformed /proc/stat"
        );
        
        let malformed_samples = vec![
            ("empty", ""),
            ("no_cpu_line", "other stuff"),
            ("truncated", "cpu  10132153"),
            ("non_numeric", "cpu  abc def ghi jkl"),
            ("negative", "cpu  -100 -200 -300 -400"),
        ];
        
        for (name, sample) in malformed_samples {
            debug!(
                test = "test_parse_proc_stat_malformed_input",
                case = name,
                sample_len = sample.len()
            );
            
            let result = parse_proc_stat(sample);
            
            info!(
                test = "test_parse_proc_stat_malformed_input",
                case = name,
                is_err = result.is_err(),
                error_msg = result.as_ref().err().map(|e| e.to_string())
            );
            
            assert!(result.is_err(), "case '{}' should fail", name);
        }
        
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_stat_malformed_input",
            phase = "complete",
            status = "PASSED",
            cases_tested = malformed_samples.len()
        );
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_parse_proc_meminfo() {
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_meminfo",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing /proc/meminfo parsing"
        );
        
        let sample = r#"MemTotal:       16384000 kB
MemFree:         8192000 kB
MemAvailable:   10240000 kB
Buffers:          512000 kB
Cached:          2048000 kB
SwapTotal:       4096000 kB
SwapFree:        4096000 kB"#;
        
        debug!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_meminfo",
            phase = "input",
            fields = vec!["MemTotal", "MemFree", "MemAvailable", "Buffers", "Cached"]
        );
        
        let mem = parse_proc_meminfo(sample).expect("parsing should succeed");
        
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_meminfo",
            phase = "assert",
            total_kb = mem.total_kb,
            available_kb = mem.available_kb,
            used_pct = format!("{:.1}%", 100.0 * (1.0 - mem.available_kb as f64 / mem.total_kb as f64))
        );
        
        assert_eq!(mem.total_kb, 16384000);
        assert_eq!(mem.available_kb, 10240000);
        
        info!(test = "test_parse_proc_meminfo", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_parse_proc_diskstats() {
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_diskstats",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing /proc/diskstats parsing for disk I/O"
        );
        
        let sample = r#"   8       0 sda 12345 6789 1000000 50000 5432 2100 500000 25000 0 30000 75000
   8       1 sda1 10000 5000 800000 40000 4000 1800 400000 20000 0 25000 60000
 253       0 nvme0n1 50000 0 2000000 100000 30000 0 1500000 80000 0 120000 180000"#;
        
        debug!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_diskstats",
            phase = "input",
            devices = vec!["sda", "sda1", "nvme0n1"]
        );
        
        let disks = parse_proc_diskstats(sample).expect("parsing should succeed");
        
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_diskstats",
            phase = "assert",
            sda_reads = disks.get("sda").map(|d| d.reads_completed),
            sda_sectors_read = disks.get("sda").map(|d| d.sectors_read),
            nvme_reads = disks.get("nvme0n1").map(|d| d.reads_completed)
        );
        
        assert_eq!(disks.get("sda").unwrap().reads_completed, 12345);
        assert_eq!(disks.get("sda").unwrap().sectors_read, 1000000);
        assert_eq!(disks.get("nvme0n1").unwrap().reads_completed, 50000);
        
        info!(test = "test_parse_proc_diskstats", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_parse_proc_net_dev() {
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_net_dev",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing /proc/net/dev parsing for network I/O"
        );
        
        let sample = r#"Inter-|   Receive                                                |  Transmit
 face |bytes    packets errs drop fifo frame compressed multicast|bytes    packets errs drop fifo colls carrier compressed
    lo: 1234567    12345    0    0    0     0          0         0  1234567    12345    0    0    0     0       0          0
  eth0: 98765432  987654    0    0    0     0          0         0 45678901   456789    0    0    0     0       0          0
 bond0: 198765432 1987654   5    2    0     0          0         0 95678901   956789    0    0    0     0       0          0"#;
        
        let net = parse_proc_net_dev(sample).expect("parsing should succeed");
        
        info!(
            test = "test_parse_proc_net_dev",
            phase = "assert",
            interfaces_found = net.interfaces.len(),
            eth0_rx_bytes = net.interfaces.get("eth0").map(|i| i.rx_bytes),
            eth0_tx_bytes = net.interfaces.get("eth0").map(|i| i.tx_bytes)
        );
        
        assert_eq!(net.interfaces["eth0"].rx_bytes, 98765432);
        assert_eq!(net.interfaces["eth0"].tx_bytes, 45678901);
        assert!(net.interfaces.contains_key("lo"));
        assert!(net.interfaces.contains_key("bond0"));
        
        info!(test = "test_parse_proc_net_dev", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
}
```

### 2. Metric Calculation Tests with Logging
```rust
// rch-telemetry/src/collect/metrics_tests.rs

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use tracing::{info, debug};
    use tracing_test::traced_test;
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_cpu_percentage_calculation() {
        info!(
            test = "test_cpu_percentage_calculation",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Verifying CPU percentage calculated from delta"
        );
        
        let prev = CpuStats { 
            user: 1000, 
            nice: 0,
            system: 500, 
            idle: 8500, 
            iowait: 0,
            irq: 0,
            softirq: 0,
        };
        let curr = CpuStats { 
            user: 1100, 
            nice: 0,
            system: 550, 
            idle: 8850, 
            iowait: 0,
            irq: 0,
            softirq: 0,
        };
        
        debug!(
            test = "test_cpu_percentage_calculation",
            prev_total = 1000 + 500 + 8500,
            curr_total = 1100 + 550 + 8850,
            delta_total = 500,
            delta_active = 150
        );
        
        let pct = calculate_cpu_percent(&prev, &curr);
        
        info!(
            test = "test_cpu_percentage_calculation",
            phase = "assert",
            expected = "30% (150/500)",
            actual = format!("{:.1}%", pct),
            tolerance = 0.1
        );
        
        assert!(
            (pct - 30.0).abs() < 0.1,
            "CPU percentage should be ~30%, got {:.1}%",
            pct
        );
        
        info!(test = "test_cpu_percentage_calculation", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_memory_pressure_calculation() {
        info!(
            test = "test_memory_pressure_calculation",
            phase = "setup"
        );
        
        let mem = MemoryInfo { 
            total_kb: 16_000_000, 
            available_kb: 4_000_000,
            free_kb: 2_000_000,
            buffers_kb: 500_000,
            cached_kb: 1_500_000,
        };
        
        let pressure = calculate_memory_pressure(&mem);
        
        info!(
            test = "test_memory_pressure_calculation",
            phase = "assert",
            total_gb = 16.0,
            available_gb = 4.0,
            used_pct = 75.0,
            calculated_pressure = pressure
        );
        
        assert!((pressure - 75.0).abs() < 0.1);
        
        info!(test = "test_memory_pressure_calculation", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_disk_io_rate_calculation() {
        info!(
            test = "test_disk_io_rate_calculation",
            phase = "setup"
        );
        
        let t0 = Instant::now();
        let prev = DiskStats { 
            sectors_read: 1_000_000, 
            sectors_written: 500_000,
            timestamp: t0,
        };
        let curr = DiskStats { 
            sectors_read: 1_100_000, 
            sectors_written: 550_000,
            timestamp: t0 + Duration::from_secs(1),
        };
        
        debug!(
            test = "test_disk_io_rate_calculation",
            delta_sectors = 100_000,
            delta_time_secs = 1,
            sector_size_bytes = 512
        );
        
        let rate_mbps = calculate_disk_read_rate(&prev, &curr);
        
        info!(
            test = "test_disk_io_rate_calculation",
            phase = "assert",
            expected_mbps = 51.2,
            actual_mbps = rate_mbps,
            calculation = "100000 * 512 / 1 / 1024 / 1024"
        );
        
        assert!((rate_mbps - 51.2).abs() < 0.1);
        
        info!(test = "test_disk_io_rate_calculation", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
}
```

### 3. Edge Case Tests with Logging
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod edge_case_tests {
    use super::*;
    use tracing::{info, warn, debug};
    use tracing_test::traced_test;
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_zero_time_delta() {
        info!(
            test = "test_zero_time_delta",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing graceful handling of zero time delta"
        );
        
        let stats = CpuStats::default();
        
        warn!(
            test = "test_zero_time_delta",
            phase = "execute",
            warning = "Zero time delta - should return 0.0 without panic"
        );
        
        let rate = calculate_cpu_percent(&stats, &stats);
        
        info!(
            test = "test_zero_time_delta",
            phase = "assert",
            result = rate,
            expected = 0.0
        );
        
        assert_eq!(rate, 0.0);
        
        info!(test = "test_zero_time_delta", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_counter_overflow() {
        info!(
            test = "test_counter_overflow",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing u64 counter overflow handling"
        );
        
        let prev = CpuStats { 
            user: u64::MAX - 100,
            system: 0,
            idle: 0,
            ..Default::default()
        };
        let curr = CpuStats { 
            user: 50,  // Wrapped around
            system: 0,
            idle: 0,
            ..Default::default()
        };
        
        warn!(
            test = "test_counter_overflow",
            phase = "execute",
            prev_user = u64::MAX - 100,
            curr_user = 50,
            note = "Counter wrapped from near-max to small value"
        );
        
        let pct = calculate_cpu_percent(&prev, &curr);
        
        info!(
            test = "test_counter_overflow",
            phase = "assert",
            result = pct,
            in_valid_range = pct >= 0.0 && pct <= 100.0
        );
        
        assert!(pct >= 0.0 && pct <= 100.0, "Result should be in valid range");
        
        info!(test = "test_counter_overflow", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
    
    #[traced_test]
    #[test]
    fn test_missing_network_interface() {
        info!(
            test = "test_missing_network_interface",
            phase = "setup",
            description = "Testing interface that disappeared between samples"
        );
        
        let mut prev_interfaces = HashMap::new();
        prev_interfaces.insert("eth0".to_string(), InterfaceStats::default());
        prev_interfaces.insert("eth1".to_string(), InterfaceStats::default());
        
        let mut curr_interfaces = HashMap::new();
        curr_interfaces.insert("eth0".to_string(), InterfaceStats::default());
        // eth1 missing!
        
        warn!(
            test = "test_missing_network_interface",
            phase = "execute",
            prev_interfaces = vec!["eth0", "eth1"],
            curr_interfaces = vec!["eth0"],
            missing = vec!["eth1"]
        );
        
        let prev = NetworkStats { interfaces: prev_interfaces };
        let curr = NetworkStats { interfaces: curr_interfaces };
        
        let rate = calculate_network_rate(&prev, &curr);
        
        info!(
            test = "test_missing_network_interface",
            phase = "assert",
            eth0_present = rate.contains_key("eth0"),
            eth1_present = rate.contains_key("eth1")
        );
        
        assert!(rate.contains_key("eth0"));
        assert!(!rate.contains_key("eth1"));  // Should be absent, not panic
        
        info!(test = "test_missing_network_interface", phase = "complete", status = "PASSED");
    }
}
```

## Test Data Fixtures
Create fixtures at `rch-telemetry/tests/fixtures/`:
- `proc_stat_sample.txt` - Representative /proc/stat output
- `proc_stat_idle.txt` - System at idle
- `proc_stat_busy.txt` - System under load
- `proc_meminfo_sample.txt` - Normal memory state
- `proc_meminfo_low.txt` - Low memory condition
- `proc_diskstats_sample.txt` - Multiple disks
- `proc_net_dev_sample.txt` - Multiple interfaces

## Running Tests with Logging

```bash
# Run all telemetry collection tests with debug logging
RUST_LOG=debug cargo test --package rch-telemetry collect -- --nocapture

# Run specific test with trace logging
RUST_LOG=trace cargo test --package rch-telemetry test_parse_proc_stat -- --nocapture

# Run tests and save logs to file
RUST_LOG=debug cargo test --package rch-telemetry 2>&1 | tee telemetry_test_logs.json

# Parse specific test results
cat telemetry_test_logs.json | jq 'select(.fields.test == "test_parse_proc_stat")'
```
